title: LLM Application Health Checklist
concept: Maintain active dashboard tracking: TTFT, cost-per-user, refusal rates, and semantic drift metrics. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Building LLM-Powered Products.'

exercises.prompt: What is an LLM Application Health Checklist?

options.text: A list of hardware parts to buy for the servers.
A set of active metrics like speed, cost, and drift tracked on a dashboard.
A spell-check tool for the code.

exercises.explain: An LLM application health checklist is tracked on an active dashboard to monitor speed, costs, and quality over time.

cards.title: Is Your AI App Healthy?
cards.body: Once you launch an AI app, you can't just set it and forget it! AI models can slow down, get more expensive, or even start giving weird, drifted answers over time. To keep your app healthy, you need a continuous checkup plan.

cards.title: LLM Health Checklist
cards.body: An LLM Health Checklist is a set of active metrics tracked on a dashboard to keep your AI application running smoothly. This includes TTFT (Time to First Token/speed), cost-per-user, refusal rates (how often the AI says 'I can't answer that'), and semantic drift (how much the AI's answers are shifting away from what we expect over time).

cards.title: The Restaurant Kitchen
cards.body: Think of your AI application like a busy restaurant kitchen. You wouldn't just taste the food once on opening night and walk away! You need to constantly check how fast plates are leaving the kitchen (TTFT), how much ingredients cost per customer (cost-per-user), how often the chef refuses to make a dish (refusal rates), and if the recipes are slowly changing taste over time (semantic drift).

cards.title: Health Check in Action
cards.body: Imagine your chatbot suddenly starts taking 10 seconds to reply instead of 1. By looking at your health dashboard, you immediately see the TTFT (speed) spike and notice that a new model update is costing 5 times more per user. You can revert the update before users get frustrated!

cards.title: The 'Set-it-and-Forget-it' Myth
cards.body: Many developers think that once an AI prompt works during testing, it will work perfectly forever. In reality, models get updated behind the scenes, user behaviors change, and systems drift. Continuous monitoring is essential to catch these silent shifts.

cards.title: Healthy vs. Unmonitored AI
cards.body: Compare a healthy AI application that is actively monitored with an unmonitored application.

left.text: Fast response speed (TTFT), low refusal rates, steady costs, and alerts for semantic drift.

right.text: Slow responses, surprise bills, random refusals, and silently worsening answer quality.

exercises.prompt: What does the health metric 'TTFT' stand for?
exercises.explain: TTFT stands for Time to First Token. It measures how long a user has to wait before the AI starts typing its response, which is crucial for a fast-feeling experience.

exercises.prompt: Tracking cost-per-user is part of an LLM health checklist.
exercises.explain: Keeping track of cost-per-user helps you ensure your application remains affordable to run as you get more users.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe when an AI's answers gradually change in meaning over time.
exercises.sentence: When an AI's responses slowly change and lose their original quality, we call it semantic drift.
exercises.explain: Semantic drift occurs when the meaning and quality of the AI's answers slowly shift away from the original goals over time.

exercises.prompt: Which of these chatbots is showing a high 'refusal rate' issue?

options.text: User: 'Tell me a joke.' AI: 'Why did the robot go on vacation? It needed to recharge!'
User: 'Write a poem about trees.' AI: 'I am sorry, but I cannot assist with writing poems about trees.'
User: 'How do I bake a potato?' AI: 'Wash it, poke it with a fork, and bake at 400 degrees!'

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an AI that is experiencing high refusal rates, repeatedly denying requests instead of helping the user.

exercises.prompt: Your LLM application has been running for a month! How should you manage its long-term health?

options.text: We only need to test the model once before launching it to the public.
Monitoring costs is important, but we can safely ignore response quality over time.
We must continuously track performance, costs, refusals, and drift on an active dashboard.
Semantic drift cannot be measured, so we should focus only on response speed.

exercises.explain: Monitoring an LLM application is an ongoing journey that tracks speed, cost, safety, and drift to ensure users always get the best experience. Next up: Building LLM-Powered Products.
